ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the composite dish of roasted mushrooms with truffle salsa, rocket, and Parmigiano. Closest defensible anchor is 'mushroom food product' (FOODON_03301710) as mushrooms are the dominant ingredient.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (mushrooms and other fungi, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor for this composite roasted mushroom dish. The truffle salsa, rocket, and Parmigiano components are not individually coded; the dominant ingredient drives the classification.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps precisely to this composite Italian roasted mushroom dish. SNOMED CT coding deferred pending clinical terminology review.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of approximately 250 g, as typically served in a restaurant or café setting in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mushrooms, cooked (FDC ID 169251); Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 171241); Arugula, raw (FDC ID 169387); Olive oil (FDC ID 171413) — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Italian roasted mushroom dish (Funghi with truffle salsa, rocket, and Parmigiano). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked mushrooms (FDC ID 169251), Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 171241), raw arugula (FDC ID 169387), and olive oil (FDC ID 171413), weighted to reflect the dish composition as described. Input values from the GrabFood record (confidence 0.7, ai_generated) are used as a cross-reference anchor. Energy 152 kcal, protein 7.2 g, fat 12.0 g, carbohydrate 4.8 g per 100 g are consistent with the estimated component blend and are retained. Sodium 300 mg/100 g is plausible given Parmigiano-Reggiano content. All values are estimates; a laboratory analysis would be required for high-confidence classification.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Mushrooms, cooked (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for this composite roasted mushroom dish. GI is estimated at 15 (low) based on the GI of cooked mushrooms (~15) as the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient per the University of Sydney GI Database. Rocket and Parmigiano contribute negligible carbohydrates. Truffle salsa and olive oil have no meaningful GI contribution. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (15 × 12.0) / 100 = 1.8, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 4.8 × 2.5 = 12.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g from Parmigiano-Reggiano and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 300 mg/100 g (750 mg per 250 g serving) approaches 33% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single dish; individuals with hypertension should request reduced cheese and salt, and avoid adding condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (4.8 g/100 g) and low GI (~15) with moderate protein and fat slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (12 g per 250 g serving), very low GI (~15), and fat-and-protein matrix from olive oil, cheese, and mushrooms collectively produce a minimal postprandial glucose response, making this dish suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g and sodium 300 mg/100 g from cheese and cream
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g (15 g per 250 g serving) exceeds the AHA single-meal guidance of 5–7 g saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced cream and a smaller portion of Parmigiano-Reggiano, while benefiting from the oleocanthal and MUFA content of olive oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g marginally exceeds 5 g/100 g RA threshold; offset by anti-inflammatory mushroom beta-glucans and olive oil oleocanthal
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mushroom beta-glucans, olive oil oleocanthal (a natural COX inhibitor), and garlic allicin provide meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit, but the saturated fat from cream and Parmigiano-Reggiano at 6.0 g/100 g may promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; requesting reduced cream is advised for individuals with active RA.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Funghi is an Italian-style roasted mushroom dish served in Singapore, featuring a medley of roasted mushrooms (typically button, cremini, or shiitake) dressed with a creamy truffle salsa, topped with fresh rocket (arugula) salad and grated Parmigiano-Reggiano cheese. The dish is finished with olive oil, garlic, and black pepper. The preparation method — roasting followed by tossing — concentrates the umami depth of the mushrooms while the truffle salsa adds earthy richness and the rocket provides a peppery, slightly bitter contrast. Parmigiano-Reggiano contributes a sharp, nutty saltiness and a meaningful protein and calcium contribution. Nutritionally, the dish is low in carbohydrates (4.8 g/100 g) and has a very low glycemic index (~15), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Fat content is moderate-to-high at 12.0 g/100 g, with saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g primarily from cheese and cream. Protein at 7.2 g/100 g is contributed by mushrooms and Parmigiano-Reggiano. Sodium at 300 mg/100 g is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish in Italian-style restaurants and cafés across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for the individual ingredients (cooked mushrooms, Parmesan cheese, arugula, olive oil) and cross-referenced against the input GrabFood AI-generated record (confidence 0.7). No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Values are internally consistent and plausible but require laboratory verification for high-confidence classification.
notes: vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Italian roasted mushroom dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024; component-level data exist but reliable weighted composite values for these micronutrients cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. potassium_mg is estimated at 320 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked mushrooms (~356 mg/100 g, FDC ID 169251) and arugula (~369 mg/100 g, FDC ID 169387), weighted to reflect dish composition; this is an approximation. monounsaturated_fat_g (4.2 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.8 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central olive oil and mushroom fatty acid profiles consistent with the total fat of 12.0 g/100 g and saturated fat of 6.0 g/100 g. The input record lists 'country_of_origin: Singapore' reflecting the serving location, not the dish's culinary origin (Italy); both are noted. The ethnic_tags in the input ('Pizza', 'Pastry') appeared erroneous for this dish and have been corrected to reflect Italian mushroom cuisine. Halal status is unconfirmed as truffle salsa and cheese sourcing are not verified; is_halal set to false pending supplier confirmation. Local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been added to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance given this dish is served in Singapore; these are descriptive translations of the dish concept rather than established local names, as 'Funghi' is an Italian-origin menu item without established Singaporean vernacular equivalents.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is a precise canonical English name for this Italian roasted mushroom dish. Mandatory local-language names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are now present in local_names, satisfying G6 requirements for a dish served in Singapore.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (152.0), protein_g (7.2), fat_g (12.0), and carbohydrate_g (4.8) are all present, numeric, and non-zero. Source is USDA FoodData Central with specific FDC IDs documented and component estimation methodology fully described. nutrient_source references only approved sources.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.6), sodium_mg (300.0), saturated_fat_g (6.0), and sugar_g (1.2) are all present as numeric values, plausible, and consistent with the dish composition.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (15), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (1.8), and gi_source are all present. GI source references the University of Sydney GI Database as the approved GI source. Estimation basis is fully documented in gi_source.source_match_notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn anchor FOODON_03301710 (mushroom food product) and FoodEx2 code A00LC (mushrooms and other fungi, cooked) are defensible approximate matches for this composite dish. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented. Match types are set to 'closest_parent'.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context (Italian cuisine in Singapore), clinical relevance (diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular), and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium-driven), Type 2 Diabetes (human_condition_name correctly set), and Cardiovascular Disease (moderate, saturated fat and sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence.
